title: Lesson 7: How to Study the Bible
description: You have the map and you know the story. Now it is time to learn the skill. This lesson teaches you the OIA method, Observe, Interpret, Apply, and then puts you to work practicing it on Psalm 23. The biblical timeline widget helps you place any passage in its historical context, and the heart of this lesson is spent with your Bible open and your pen in hand.

blocks.text: In Lesson 5 you explored the Bible as a library of 66 books organized by type. In Lesson 6 you traced the one story it tells, from creation to new creation. You now have the map and you know where the road leads. This lesson hands you the skill to dig into any passage on your own.

blocks.alt: Philip running alongside the Ethiopian's chariot to explain the Scriptures

blocks.title: Philip and the Ethiopian
blocks.text:
In Acts 8, an Ethiopian official was riding home from Jerusalem. He was an important man, a court treasurer, and he had traveled a long way to worship God. Now on the dusty road home he had the scroll of Isaiah unrolled in front of him, reading aloud, hungry for truth but unable to make sense of what he saw. God sent Philip running to the side of the chariot with one simple question:

"So Philip ran to him and heard him reading Isaiah the prophet and asked, 'Do you understand what you are reading?' And he said, 'How can I, unless someone guides me?'" (Acts 8:30-31)

Philip climbed in, sat beside him, and helped him see what was already on the page. He did not add to Scripture. He showed the man how to read it. That is what Bible study is. It is not reading faster or reading more. It is learning to see what is there, understand what it means, and let it shape how you live.
Three Steps: Observe, Interpret, Apply

Faithful Bible students have used this method for generations. It is called OIA: Observe, Interpret, and Apply. Think of it as three questions asked in order. What does the text say? What does the text mean? What does this mean for my life? The order matters. Most people jump straight to application, but the richest application always comes after you have done the honest work of observation and interpretation first.
Observe: What Does the Text Say?

Before you can understand what a passage means, you need to see what it actually says. Read the passage more than once. Ask: Who is speaking? Who is listening? What is happening? What words or phrases are repeated? What surprises you? Do not interpret yet. Just observe. Many of the richest discoveries in Scripture come from simply paying attention to details that everyone else skips past.

Context is one of the most powerful tools you have when observing, and the last two lessons gave you two of the three pieces you need. Lesson 5 taught you what type of book you are reading, because a psalm is not a legal code and a parable is not a historical report. Lesson 6 gave you the biblical storyline, so you can see where a passage fits in the four movements of Creation, Fall, Redemption, and Restoration. The timeline below gives you the third piece: when it happened. Knowing the era, who was in power, and what God's people were going through helps you notice things you would otherwise miss.
Interpret: What Does the Text Mean?

Once you have observed what the text says, ask what it means. Here is the most important rule of interpretation: a passage cannot mean today what it never meant to the original audience. Before you ask what it means for you, ask what it meant to them. What was the author trying to communicate? How does this passage fit in the larger chapter and book? How does it connect to the storyline of Scripture? This is where your map, your storyline, and your timeline all come together. Let the Bible interpret the Bible. Often the clearest explanation of a passage is found somewhere else in Scripture, and the more you read, the more those connections will light up.
Apply: What Does This Mean for My Life?

After you have observed what the text says and interpreted what it means, ask how this truth reshapes the way you live. Is there a truth to believe? A command to obey? A sin to confess? A promise to trust? An example to follow? How does this passage point you to Jesus? The goal is not just knowledge but transformation. The Bible was never meant to sit on a shelf. It was meant to get into your bones and change the way you walk through your day.
Do your best to present yourself to God as one approved, a worker who has no need to be ashamed, rightly handling the word of truth.

Notice the word "worker." Good Bible study takes effort, but the effort is worth it. When you rightly handle the Word of truth, the Bible stops being a confusing book you feel guilty about not reading and becomes a living conversation with the God who loves you.
7.1 - Which of the three steps (Observe, Interpret, Apply) feels most new to you? Which one do you think you tend to skip?
Practice: Psalm 23

The best way to learn this method is to use it. Below is one of the most beloved passages in all of Scripture. You may already know the opening line by heart, but watch what happens when you slow down and walk through it with OIA.

The Lord is my shepherd; I shall not want. He makes me lie down in green pastures. He leads me beside still waters. He restores my soul. He leads me in paths of righteousness for his name's sake. Even though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, I will fear no evil, for you are with me; your rod and your staff, they comfort me. You prepare a table before me in the presence of my enemies; you anoint my head with oil; my cup overflows. Surely goodness and mercy shall follow me all the days of my life, and I shall dwell in the house of the Lord forever.
Observe

Use the timeline above to place this psalm. David wrote it around 1000 BC, during the era of the kings. Before he was king of Israel, David was a literal shepherd who spent years in the fields with real sheep, leading them to food and water, protecting them from predators, using his rod to fight off wolves and his staff to pull wandering sheep back to the path. Knowing this changes everything about how you read the opening line.
7.2 - Read Psalm 23 again slowly. What do you notice? What actions does the shepherd take? What words or images stand out? What shifts between the first three verses and verse four?
Interpret

7.3 - Knowing that David was a real shepherd who tended real sheep, what does it mean that he calls God his shepherd? What is he saying about God's character and care? How does the "valley of the shadow of death" connect to what you saw in the biblical storyline?
Apply

7.4 - What does this psalm call you to believe about God in your current season of life? Is there a fear you need to lay down because the Shepherd is with you? What is one way you can rest in His care this week?

You just studied the Bible. You observed what was on the page, placed it in its historical context, interpreted what it meant, and applied it to your life. That is not something only pastors and scholars can do. It is a skill you now have, and you can use it with any passage, any day, for the rest of your life.
A Skill Worth Sharing

Everything you have learned in these three lessons is transferable: the map of the Bible, the four-part storyline, the timeline, and the OIA method. You could sit down with a friend this week, open the Bible to any passage, and walk them through the same steps you just practiced. Remember the Ethiopian? He needed a Philip, someone willing to sit beside him and say, "Let me show you what I have learned about reading this book." Someday soon, someone in your life will need that from you. And when that day comes, you will be ready.

Before you move on, pick a passage of Scripture this week and walk through OIA on your own. It does not need to be long. A few verses are enough. Open your Bible, slow down, and see what God shows you.
7.5 - What passage did you choose, and what were your takeaways from walking through Observe, Interpret, and Apply on your own?
Lesson 7 Review

You now have two tools for opening the Bible. The Three Discovery Questions from the Starting course are your daily method, simple enough to use every time you read. The OIA method is your deeper study tool, designed for when you want to slow down and dig into a passage with care. The Discovery Questions are your daily bread. OIA is your weekly feast. Use both, and the Word of God will come alive in your hands.
Closing Reflections
What is one key thing you learned from this lesson, and what next step can you take this week?
What have you learned about God and yourself recently in your Daily Devotions?
What is a prayer request for this week?
